title: Getting Started
text: Sometimes helpful subset CDM specific set persons simply sample data reasonable size. Let’s subset cdm just persons Pneumonia (concept_id 255848). works best number persons subset quite small database indexes “person_id” columns table. Alternatively simply want random sample entire CDM can use cdm_sample.
code: personIds <- cdm$condition_occurrence %>%    filter(condition_concept_id == 255848) %>%    distinct(person_id) %>%    pull(person_id)  length(personIds) #> [1] 52  cdm_pneumonia <- cdm %>%   cdmSubset(personId = personIds)  tally(cdm_pneumonia$person) %>%    pull(n) #> [1] 52  cdm_pneumonia$condition_occurrence %>%    distinct(person_id) %>%    tally() %>%    pull(n) #> [1] 52 cdm_100person <- cdmSample(cdm, n = 100)  tally(cdm_100person$person) %>% pull("n") #> [1] 100

title: Working with cohorts
text: First need create CDM object. Note need specify write_schema creating object. Cohort tables go CDM’s write_schema. generated cohort associated metadata tables. can access utility functions. cohort_count contains person record counts cohort cohort set settings table contains cohort id cohort name attrition table contains attrition information (persons, records dropped sequential inclusion rule) Note cohort table still database can quite large. can also join CDM table subset entire cdm just persons cohort.
code: library(CDMConnector) pathToCohortJsonFiles <- system.file("example_cohorts", package = "CDMConnector") list.files(pathToCohortJsonFiles)  con <- DBI::dbConnect(duckdb::duckdb(), eunomiaDir("GiBleed")) cdm <- cdmFromCon(con, cdmName = "eunomia", cdmSchema = "main", writeSchema = "main")  cohortSet <- readCohortSet(pathToCohortJsonFiles) |>   mutate(cohort_name = snakecase::to_snake_case(cohort_name))  cohortSet  cdm <- generateCohortSet(   cdm = cdm,    cohortSet = cohortSet,   name = "study_cohorts" )  cdm$study_cohorts cohortCount(cdm$study_cohorts) settings(cdm$study_cohorts) attrition(cdm$study_cohorts) cdm_gibleed <- cdm %>%    cdmSubsetCohort(cohortTable = "study_cohorts")

N cohorts, database performs: N vocabulary expansions (CONCEPT_ANCESTOR joins) N scans per CDM domain table used N DELETE + INSERT cycles result table

title: DAG-Based Batch Optimization for Cohort SQL Generation
text: Cohorts share CDM domains (e.g., multiple drug-exposure cohorts, multiple condition-based cohorts) see largest benefit: Domain tables scanned : 500M-row DRUG_EXPOSURE table filtered ##DRUG_EXPOSURE_FILTERED, cohorts read smaller filtered table Concept overlap: cohorts share concepts (common phenotype libraries), filtered tables even smaller Typical speedup: 3–10x 10–50 cohorts sharing 2–3 domains. savings grow roughly linearly N additional cohort avoids full CDM scan.

what: Appendix: How the DAG Is Enforced
title: DAG-Based Batch Optimization for Cohort SQL Generation
text: batch optimizer use explicit graph data structure. Instead, DAG enforced SQL execution order: assembled script linear sequence SQL statements phase references tables created earlier phases. back-edges (later phases never write tables read earlier phases) cross-cohort dependencies (cohort’s Phase 2 block reads another cohort’s Phase 2 output). linear ordering valid topological sort implicit DAG: Phase 2 blocks independent, principle parallelized. current implementation executes sequentially within single SQL batch, DAG structure means ordering constraint exists different cohorts’ Phase 2 blocks.
code: #CODESETS_GLOBAL     |     v #ALL_CONCEPTS     |     +----> ##DRUG_EXPOSURE_FILTERED     +----> ##CONDITION_OCCURRENCE_FILTERED     +----> ##PROCEDURE_OCCURRENCE_FILTERED     +----> ... (other domains)     +----> ##ATLAS_OBSERVATION_PERIOD            |            v     #QUALIFIED_EVENTS_ALL  (Phase 1: per-cohort inserts, shared table)            |            v     Per-cohort Phase 2 blocks  (independent of each other)            |            v     Staging tables (#COHORT_STAGE, ...)            |            v     Finalize (results tables)

title: Incremental DAG Caching for Cohort Generation
text: iterating cohort definitions, often change one part definition re-run. Without caching, entire pipeline re-executes scratch: concept set expansion, primary events, qualified events, inclusion rules, final cohort construction – even parts haven’t changed. atlasCohortGenerator implements incremental DAG caching: system persists intermediate computation tables database skips recomputation unchanged nodes. principle behind build systems like Make Bazel – content-addressable caching automatic invalidation via Merkle-tree hashing. vignette explains: caching works (Merkle-tree hashing, registry, cache-aware execution) enable caching workflow gets cached vs. ’s always recomputed Cache management (inspection, garbage collection, clearing) Correctness guarantees

title: Incremental DAG Caching for Cohort Generation
text: cohort definition decomposed directed acyclic graph (DAG) typed computation nodes: node identified content hash – deterministic 16-character hex string derived node’s definition. Two nodes definition produce hash, regardless cohort came .
code: concept_set (CS)        | primary_events (PE)        | qualified_events (QE)        | inclusion_rule (IR) [0..N]        | included_events (IE)        | cohort_exit (CE)        | final_cohort (FC)

title: Incremental DAG Caching for Cohort Generation
text: critical property makes caching safe Merkle-tree hashing: node’s hash incorporates hashes dependencies, just definition. example, qualified_events node’s hash computed : parameters (qualified limit type, sort order) hash parent primary_events node hash additional criteria group means change concept set, change propagates entire DAG: explicit invalidation logic needed. change anywhere automatically produces different hashes downstream nodes.
code: CS (concept_id=123) --> hash: a1b2c3d4...   PE (uses CS hash) --> hash: e5f6a7b8...     QE (uses PE hash) --> hash: 1234abcd...       ...  CS (concept_id=999) --> hash: ff00ee11...   <-- changed   PE (uses CS hash) --> hash: 2233aabb...   <-- also changed (different dep hash)     QE (uses PE hash) --> hash: ccdd4455... <-- also changed       ...

title: Incremental DAG Caching for Cohort Generation
text: caching enabled, node tables use fixed prefix (dagcache_) instead random atlas_<uuid>_ prefix used ephemeral runs. means computation always maps table name: dagcache_pe_a1b2c3d4 – primary events node hash starting a1b2c3d4 dagcache_qe_e5f6a7b8 – qualified events node hash starting e5f6a7b8 determinism allows cache lookups work across separate sessions.

title: Incremental DAG Caching for Cohort Generation
text: run cache = TRUE, execution proceeds follows: Build DAG cohort definitions (non-cached mode) Compute content hashes nodes (Merkle-tree, bottom-) Query registry node hash Validate cached tables still physically exist database Skip nodes valid cache hits Execute SQL cache misses Register newly computed nodes registry Clean ephemeral tables (staging, domain-filtered), preserve cached node tables

title: Incremental DAG Caching for Cohort Generation
text: caching system provides guarantees: Content-addressed: Two computations identical inputs always produce hash. false cache hits stale data. Merkle-tree propagation: Changing upstream definition (concept set, criteria, observation window, etc.) produces different hash every downstream node. tested directly: Physical validation: declaring cache hit, system verifies backing table still exists database. someone drops cached table externally, recomputed next run. Immutability: Cached tables never modified creation. hash promise table contents match definition. cross-contamination: final_cohort node includes cohort_id hash, two cohorts identical logic different IDs produce separate final cohort nodes (cached anyway – insert staging dropped).
code: # Changing concept_id from 123 to 999 changes ALL downstream hashes dag_a <- build_execution_dag(cohort_a, ...) dag_b <- build_execution_dag(cohort_b, ...) # PE, QE, IE, CE, FC hashes all differ between dag_a and dag_b

title: Incremental DAG Caching for Cohort Generation
text: Caching beneficial : Iterating cohort definitions: changing one inclusion rule set 20 cohorts → affected nodes recompute Adding/removing cohorts batch: unchanged cohorts reuse cached intermediates Re-running failed partial execution: successfully computed nodes persist even errors Working large CDM databases: primary events qualified events scans expensive; caching avoids repeating Caching adds minimal overhead (registry lookups fast) can disabled time omitting cache = TRUE.

what: Performance improvements with the new approach
title: Multi-Database Benchmarking: Old vs New Cohort Generation
text: new approach (generateCohortSet2) uses DAG-based batch optimizer : Shares vocabulary expansion: Concept set expansion codeset building done reused across cohorts, instead per cohort. Shares domain scans: Filtered domain tables (e.g. drug exposure, condition occurrence) built read every cohort needs . Reduces /O: single batch script writes shared staging tables finalizes one pass, instead N separate DELETE/INSERT cycles. result, wall-clock time typically decreases number cohorts overlap concept sets increase. ratio (new time / old time) often 1.0, larger batches showing greater speedups. exact improvement depends : Number cohorts size definition Overlap concept sets domains across cohorts Database engine hardware benchmarking script records time_old_sec, time_new_sec, ratio_new_over_old per database can measure speedup data platforms.

what: Get similar concepts from Columbia Open Health Data (COHD) API — cohdSimilarConcepts
title: Get similar concepts from Columbia Open Health Data (COHD) API — cohdSimilarConcepts
text: Queries COHD API association/relativeFrequency endpoint return concepts co-occur given concept(s), ranked relative frequency. Useful finding clinically related conditions, drugs, procedures based EHR prevalence. given multiple concept IDs, returns concepts co-occur input set, ranked many input concepts co-occur mean relative frequency.

title: Get similar concepts from Columbia Open Health Data (COHD) API — cohdSimilarConcepts
code: cohdSimilarConcepts(   conceptId,   datasetId = 1,   topN = 50,   timeoutSec = 30,   baseUrl = "https://cohd-api.ci.transltr.io/api" )

title: Get similar concepts from Columbia Open Health Data (COHD) API — cohdSimilarConcepts
text: conceptId Integer character vector. One OMOP concept IDs find similar concepts (e.g. conditions, drugs, procedures). datasetId Integer. COHD dataset ID (1 = 5-year, 2 = lifetime; default 1). topN Integer. Maximum number similar concepts return (default 50). single concept, limits rows strength; multiple concepts, limits aggregated result. timeoutSec Numeric. Request timeout seconds (default 30). baseUrl Character. Base URL COHD API (default "https://cohd-api.ci.transltr.io/api").

title: Get similar concepts from Columbia Open Health Data (COHD) API — cohdSimilarConcepts
text: data frame one row per similar concept, NULL API unavailable request fails. successful: Single concept: data frame contains concept_id_1, concept_id_2, concept_count_1, concept_count_2, concept_count, relative_frequency, other_concept_id; rows sorted relative_frequency descending. Multiple concepts: data frame contains other_concept_id, n_concepts (many input concepts co-occur one), mean_rf (mean relative frequency); rows sorted n_concepts descending mean_rf descending. results error occurs, returns NULL message printed.

title: Get similar concepts from Columbia Open Health Data (COHD) API — cohdSimilarConcepts
text: Ta, Casey N.; Dumontier, Michel; Hripcsak, George; P. Tatonetti, Nicholas; Weng, Chunhua (2018). Columbia Open Health Data, database EHR prevalence co-occurrence conditions, drugs, procedures. figshare. Collection. doi:10.6084/m9.figshare.c.4151252.v1

title: Get similar concepts from Columbia Open Health Data (COHD) API — cohdSimilarConcepts

title: Create a copy of an example OMOP CDM dataset — eunomiaDir
text: Eunomia datasets available CDMConnector Synthea project. Synthea open-source synthetic patient generator models medical history synthetic patients. Synthea datasets generated using Synthea tool converted OMOP CDM format using OHDSI ETL-Synthea project https://ohdsi.github.io/ETL-Synthea/. Currently synthea datasets available OMOP CDM v5.3 format. See https://synthetichealth.github.io/synthea/ details Synthea project. addition Synthea, Eunomia project provides CMS Synthetic Public Use Files (SynPUFs) 5.3 5.4 OMOP CDM formats. data synthetic US Medicare claims data mapped OMOP CDM format. OMOP CDM set optional metadata tables, called Achilles tables, include pre-computed analytics entire dataset record person counts. Eunomia Synpuf datasets include Achilles tables. Eunomia also provides empty cdms can used starting point creating new example CDM. useful creating test data studies analytic packages. empty CDM includes vocabulary tables OMOP CDM tables clinical tables empty need populated data. additional information creating small test CDM datasets see https://ohdsi.github.io/omock/ https://darwin-eu.github.io/TestGenerator/. contribute synthetic observational health data Eunomia project please open issue https://github.com/OHDSI/Eunomia/issues/ Setup: use eunomiaDir function please set EUNOMIA_DATA_FOLDER .Renviron file folder computer datasets downloaded . file can opened calling usethis::edit_r_environ().

title: Create a copy of an example OMOP CDM dataset — eunomiaDir
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{   # The defaults GiBleed dataset is a small dataset that is useful for testing  library(CDMConnector)  con <- DBI::dbConnect(duckdb::duckdb(), eunomiaDir())  cdm <- cdmFromCon(con, "main", "main")  cdmDisconnect(cdm)   # Synpuf datasets include the Achilles tables  con <- DBI::dbConnect(duckdb::duckdb(), eunomiaDir("synpuf-1k", "5.3"))  cdm <- cdmFromCon(con, "main", "main", achillesSchema = "main")  cdmDisconnect(cdm)   # Currently the only 5.4 dataset is synpuf-1k  con <- DBI::dbConnect(duckdb::duckdb(), eunomiaDir("synpuf-1k", "5.4"))  cdm <- cdmFromCon(con, "main", "main", achillesSchema = "main")  cdmDisconnect(cdm)  } # }
